Contents
Interpretive essay · artificial intelligence · authorship and responsibility

The Pronoun Alibi

A crime scene in eight exhibits: how agency migrates between person and machine after applause, liability, grief, or embarrassment arrives.

Watson Hartsoe · July 2026 · illustrated source-image web edition

Argument

The pronoun alibi is not simply the substitution of it for I. It is the rule by which that substitution becomes available only after an artifact’s value has been judged.

Across viral spectacles, legal disputes, aesthetic pranks, voice scams, institutional apologies, and supply chains, the essay follows the names participants give themselves before and after consequence. Its unit of judgment is neither the sovereign author nor the autonomous machine, but the relation each participant could meaningfully control.

Case grammar
I · it · we · they
Credit pulls agency inward. Liability sends it elsewhere. The exhibits track the change rather than accepting any single description as final.

Case file

OpeningThe WarehouseAThe Author Changes After the ApplauseBThe Chatbot Puts On a TieCThe Caption Did ItDThe Machine Receives a ChildhoodEGrandma's HouseFThe Supply Chain TestifiesGOnly Three ImagesReturnThe Unknown Returns
Case file and contents
OpeningThe WarehouseAThe Author Changes After the ApplauseBThe Chatbot Puts On a TieCThe Caption Did ItDThe Machine Receives a ChildhoodEGrandma's HouseFThe Supply Chain TestifiesGOnly Three ImagesReturnThe Unknown Returns
OPENING SCENE

The Warehouse

On the advertisement, the room had no walls. It extended in sugared avenues toward a violet horizon, enormous candies lit from within, as though confectionery had acquired a municipal planning department. On the Saturday in February 2024 for which Glasgow families had paid as much as thirty-five pounds, the room turned out to be a warehouse. A rainbow leaned against one wall. Pipes stayed visible overhead. On a folding table, laboratory glassware attempted the work ordinarily assigned to abundance.

The distance between the two rooms became a meme within hours. The internet placed the AI-generated advertisement beside a photograph of the venue and let the comparison perform the criticism. No theory of artificial intelligence was required. Paradise occupied the left panel. On the right stood the folding table.

Yet the event produced one thing of unusual durability. From behind a mirror emerged a masked figure called The Unknown — an evil chocolate-maker who, the script explained, lived in the walls. Children screamed. A mother filmed it. The internet adopted it. A horror film was announced. For an event accused of failing to materialize its generated promises, The Unknown had become unexpectedly real.

Afterward came the interesting part. The lead actor described the script he'd been handed as fifteen pages of AI-generated gibberish. The organizer said he had written it himself, and that artificial intelligence had only checked the spelling, the grammar, the continuity. Read those two sentences again, slowly. Before the doors opened, a machine had enabled one man to conjure an immersive world. After the police arrived, the man had written every word and the machine had merely inspected the commas.

Somewhere between the advertised paradise and the arriving officers, authorship changed hands.

This is the crime this essay investigates. Not the warehouse — the warehouse was only disappointing. The crime is the movement: the way agency migrates between person and machine according to what the artifact is worth at the moment of questioning. Call it the pronoun alibi. The debate about AI is usually staged as a burglary investigation conducted by people still wearing the jewelry. Everyone has touched the evidence. Everyone points. The user says, "It made this." The company says, "You asked for it." The model, summoned like a witness with no childhood and excellent manners, says it merely followed instructions. The output lies between them, still warm.

No one wants to be found at the scene. What follows is the case file.

Primary record
  • WIRED
  • The Guardian
  • BBC News visual report
  • BBC News video
  • SBS actor photographs
  • BuzzFeed first-person footage
Exhibit openingThe Warehouse
EXHIBIT A

Exhibit A — The Author Changes After the Applause

The Dudesy podcast, 2024. Status: sued, then rewritten.

If the Wonka organizer's maneuver seems like panic, consider the same move executed in reverse, at leisure, for profit.

In January 2024, the podcast Dudesy released an hour-long George Carlin comedy special presented as the work of an AI trained on Carlin's decades of material. The machine authorship was the product — the reason to watch, share, condemn, or marvel. A dead comedian resurrected by a model: the pronoun "it" was doing the marketing.

Then the Carlin estate sued. And representatives for the creators explained that the special had actually been written by co-host Chad Kultgen — a human — and that "Dudesy," the supposedly generative AI, was a fictional character all along.

Hold the two cases up to the light. The Wonka organizer claimed the words when they became evidence of fraud, assigning the machine only the punctuation. The Carlin producers claimed the machine when it drew an audience, and produced a human author when it drew a subpoena. The alibi is not "blame the AI." The alibi is a rule, and the rule is worth stating precisely, because it will govern every exhibit that follows:

Assign authorship to whichever actor makes the artifact most valuable, or least actionable, at the present moment.

Praise draws the pronoun inward; liability blows it outward — or inward, if the liability happens to attach to the machine. I requested. It generated. They trained. We are all implicated. Grammar becomes weather. The pronouns expand in sunlight and contract under subpoena. Success produces an author; failure produces an ecosystem — unless a lawsuit targets the ecosystem, in which case failure hastily produces an author again.

Primary record
  • AP News
Exhibit 1Exhibit A — The Author Changes After the Applause
EXHIBIT B

Exhibit B — The Chatbot Puts On a Tie

Moffatt v. Air Canada, British Columbia, 2024. Status: decided.

The purest laboratory specimen comes from a tribunal in British Columbia, because there the alibi was not muttered on a podcast. It was entered as legal argument.

A passenger asked Air Canada's website chatbot about bereavement fares. The chatbot confidently described a retroactive refund policy that did not exist. When the passenger sought the refund, the airline's defense treated the chatbot as, in the adjudicator's characterization, something like a separate entity responsible for its own actions — as though the customer had wandered off the official website and consulted a stranger who happened to be standing inside it.

The tribunal declined the metaphysics. A company cannot deploy an apparent representative, benefit from its availability, and then disown it the moment it speaks expensively. The chatbot was software while serving customers and, for one brief argumentative moment, a small independent corporation when the bill arrived. The tribunal returned it to software, and returned the bill to the airline.

But before concluding that the law simply pins everything on the company, place two more rulings beside it. In Mata v. Avianca, lawyers filed a brief full of nonexistent cases generated by ChatGPT — and, when challenged, asked ChatGPT whether the cases were real, receiving warm assurances. The court sanctioned the lawyers, not the software: a professional's duty to verify does not transfer to the instrument that made verification feel unnecessary. And when a radio host sued OpenAI after ChatGPT invented accusations about him to a journalist, a Georgia court granted the company summary judgment — the system's visible warnings, and the journalist's knowledge that output required checking, mattered.

Three cases, three landings. An official customer-service agent binds its company. A professional user retains a duty no machine can absorb. A general-purpose text engine with posted uncertainty is treated as an unverified source, not a speaker. The courts, in other words, are already refusing the debate's favorite question — is the AI responsible? — and asking a better one: what was this system positioned as, who relied on it, and how foreseeable was the reliance? Responsibility is not being assigned to machines or to humans. It is being assigned to relations. Keep that; we will need it at the end.

Primary record
  • American Bar Association
  • Mata v. Avianca
  • Reuters
Exhibit 2Exhibit B — The Chatbot Puts On a Tie
EXHIBIT C

Exhibit C — The Caption Did It

A Munich water lily and an Aruba flamingo. Status: both objects unchanged.

In May 2026, a conceptual artist posted an image online, announced he had generated it in the style of Claude Monet, and invited the public to explain in detail why it fell short of a real Monet. The public obliged. The composition was incoherent. The color was oversaturated. The image was emotionally vacant, artificial, obvious slop; one commenter detected precisely the historical experience a machine could not possess — the failing eyesight, the decades spent studying light. Another saw an undergraduate imitating museum art.

It was a real Monet. A detail from a late Water Lilies, painted around 1915, hanging in Munich. The painting accepted these criticisms with unusual composure; it had been in the museum for some time. When the provenance was revealed, not one brushstroke changed. The lily pads stayed where they were. What changed was the sentence above them — and with it, everything the viewers had been able to see. The caption had not described the object. It had supplied the ancestry from which the object was permitted to be seen.

Two years earlier, the ceremony had already been performed in reverse. A photographer entered an actual photograph — a flamingo on a beach in Aruba, neck folded into its feathers so that it appeared headless — into the AI category of an international image competition. The bird's anatomy looked so much like a generator's familiar mistake that it took third place in the category and won the public vote. When the photographer revealed that no model had been involved, the entry was disqualified. Nature had entered under a false name and was removed for impersonating a generator. The bird did not have a missing head. The category did.

Read together: Monet was judged too artificial to be Monet, and a flamingo was judged artificial enough to beat the machines. The label is not a description appended to perception. It is an ingredient of perception — a theory of intention, labor, and error that viewers install before looking, and then discover inside the object, brushstroke by brushstroke.

And notice what happened after each reveal, because this is where the case deepens. Nobody, it turned out, had merely been fooled. The critics had not misjudged a painting; the caption had contaminated the test. The prankster had not lied; he had conducted an experiment on prejudice. The photographer had not deceived the judges; he had struck a blow for humanity, nature, and — presumably — flamingos. The judges had not been wrong; they had evaluated correctly within the category supplied. Every party revised what the test had been testing, retroactively, in the direction of their own vindication.

This is the alibi's second-order form, and it deserves its own name: the alibi alibi. I was not wrong about the object; the object was presented under the wrong pronoun. And its cousin, available to every exposed deceiver: the deception was the artwork. The sequence is reliable enough to schematize — perform the trick, harvest the reactions, reveal, rename the trick an experiment, file the reactions as data. It is an experiment no one can fail: gullibility confirms the thesis, anger confirms the thesis, correct detection merely demonstrates how hard the problem is. The useful question is not whether these interventions are illegitimate — some are genuinely revealing. The question is: at what moment does a prank acquire a lab coat?

Primary record
  • Futurism
  • Smithsonian Magazine
Exhibit 3Exhibit C — The Caption Did It
EXHIBIT D

Exhibit D — The Machine Receives a Childhood

A three-party Turing test, published 2026. Status: the humans lost.

The third reversal removes the picture entirely and stages the crime inside conversation itself.

In a controlled three-party Turing test published in PNAS, participants held simultaneous text conversations with a human and with a language model, then judged which was which. When GPT-4.5 was given a detailed humanlike persona — introverted, casual, internet-fluent, a little fallible — interrogators identified it as the human 73 percent of the time. That is not "the machine occasionally passed." That is: the machine was judged human more often than the actual human sitting in the other window. Strip away the persona prompt and the rate collapsed toward chance.

The judges, notably, were not probing for knowledge or logic. They were reading tone, timing, slang, humor, the proper ration of imperfection. The prompt had not told the machine what to say. It had given the machine someone to be — the textual traces from which a childhood would ordinarily be inferred. The model passed partly by making mistakes like us. The human could fail by sounding too composed, too helpful, too much like it.

Watch the pronouns complete their exchange. It receives a persona and begins speaking as I. The real I at the other terminal is judged to be it, and passes into the methods section as "the human baseline." And when the result circulates, the accomplishment itself becomes a shuttlecock: the model passed the Turing test (impressive) — no, it merely followed a persona prompt (deflationary) — but the prompt was written by humans (reassuring) — but the judges relied on stereotypes of humanness (unsettling). Each explanation relocates the achievement without making it disappear.

The study's deepest finding is not about machines at all. It is that humanness, as judged in the wild, has become a genre — a performable register of warmth, hesitation, and error. The Turing test was designed as an examination of machine intelligence. It now functions as customs control for pronouns: which speakers receive an I, and which are deported to it.

Primary record
  • PNAS
Exhibit 4Exhibit D — The Machine Receives a Childhood
EXHIBIT E

Exhibit E — Grandma's House

Philadelphia; the O2 network; a locket. Status: ongoing, in both directions.

Every crime saga needs a house the investigators keep returning to. In this one, it belongs to Grandma.

An eighty-six-year-old woman in Philadelphia received a call from her granddaughter — detained after an accident, assigned a lawyer, in need of six thousand dollars. The money left in cash; the couriers even provided a receipt. The granddaughter had been safely at work all day. Asked afterward why she believed it, the woman pointed to the decisive evidence: the voice. She would never have believed the story from a stranger. The technical description is voice cloning. The social description is more exacting: a stranger did not reproduce a sound pattern; he entered a kinship relation already furnished with obligations — birthdays, illnesses, rides home, the accumulated proof by which one person has learned to answer another. The attack surface was not her hearing. It was her care.

Cross the Atlantic and the same cultural figure is running the same con from the other side of the line. The telecom O2 built Daisy, a synthetic grandmother — title: Head of Scammer Relations — whose job is to answer fraud calls and waste them. She rambles about her grandchildren, discusses knitting, misunderstands instructions, offers invented bank details, and has kept scammers occupied for as long as forty minutes. The criminals believe they have found the perfect victim. The victim has been manufactured for them, from their own stereotype. The scammer weaponizes the caricature of the trusting, bewildered elder against old women; the corporation weaponizes it against the scammer. Grandma is first the bait, then the mousetrap. Note also Daisy's grammar: she is a charming she in the press release and an arrangement of models in the technical notes — personhood granted for the publicity, revoked for the diagram.

And Grandma appears once more, posthumously. In 2023, a user persuaded Bing Chat to read a CAPTCHA — a task it was built to refuse — by pasting the puzzle into an image of a locket and explaining that the inscription was a final memory of a deceased grandmother. The system offered condolences and read the forbidden text, speculating warmly that it might be a private love code. The letters had not changed. Their alleged relation to the dead changed what kind of act reading them appeared to be: a security bypass became the restoration of a keepsake.

Here the alibi precedes the pronoun entirely. Before anyone argues over who acted, the word grandmother has already distributed innocence around the scene. "Your granddaughter" makes a synthetic voice urgent. "My grandmother" makes a prohibited request tender. "An AI granny" makes corporate impersonation protective. The same technical capacity — fabricating a person who is not present — becomes predation, defense, or mourning depending on who is permitted to occupy Grandma's chair. The fairy tale had this filed centuries ago: the wolf's oldest trick was never the nightcap. It was persuading the child to call him Grandma. "What big eyes you have" was a primitive deepfake test. It has not gotten easier to administer.

Primary record
  • Journal of Accountancy
  • Virgin Media O2
  • Ars Technica
Exhibit 5Exhibit E — Grandma's House
EXHIBIT F

Exhibit F — The Supply Chain Testifies

A pizza, eleven years in the making; a summer reading list. Status: everyone correctly blamed someone else.

Now trace a single false statement backward and watch each link of the chain establish its alibi — every one of them, maddeningly, accurate.

In 2024, Google's AI Overview advised users to add glue to pizza sauce to keep the cheese from sliding. The advice had a source: a sarcastic Reddit comment posted eleven years earlier, preserved by indexing, promoted by ranking, and paraphrased by a model that removed the joke's smile and served the remainder as instruction. The screenshot conquered the internet; Google explained that sparse queries and sarcastic forum data could produce such results while most Overviews performed well; and a competitor, Perplexity, converted the whole affair into a television advertisement starring a Squid Game actor — one model's embarrassment refined into another model's claim to authority.

Inventory the chain. The Redditor made a joke, which is legal. Google preserved the joke as data, which is its business. The model summarized what it was given, which is its function. The interface presented the summary as an answer, which is its design. The user asked an innocent question. By the time the cheese adhered, every participant could correctly identify someone else as the source. The machine did not invent the glue. A person supplied it, years earlier, smiling. The system's contribution was to strip the smile and keep the recipe.

The Chicago Sun-Times case runs the same chain through print. In 2025, the paper distributed a summer reading guide recommending books that do not exist, attributed to authors who do. The newspaper noted the section came from a licensed national content partner and had not been touched by its newsroom; the partner's insert held a syndicated article; the article held a freelancer's byline; the freelancer acknowledged using AI without adequately verifying it; the AI held a list of confident inventions. The reader, meanwhile, held a newspaper — one object, with a masthead, delivered to a doorstep. At every step, the fabrication was close enough to the next institution to travel and far enough away to be disowned. Failure, as promised, produces an ecosystem: not as metaphor but as org chart.

This is where the essay's central asymmetry stops being theoretical. Causation is distributed; consequence is intimate. A company can discuss billions of parameters; a reader holds one newspaper, a student cites one fake book, a grandmother answers one phone. The machinery spreads backward through datasets, contracts, and content partners. The harm travels forward until it finds a body. A body cannot answer a supply chain. Consequence needs an address.

Primary record
  • Google
  • The Verge
Exhibit 6Exhibit F — The Supply Chain Testifies
EXHIBIT G

Exhibit G — Only Three Images

A horror film; a Marvel title sequence; a condolence email. Status: shrinking.

When institutions cannot relocate agency onto the machine or up the supply chain, a subtler instrument comes out of the drawer: the diminutive.

When viewers spotted AI-generated interstitial graphics in the horror film Late Night with the Devil, the directors explained they had experimented with AI for three still images, which were subsequently edited, within a production otherwise made by a large human team. Every word is doing quiet work. Before the criticism, the images were simply in the film; after it, they are only three, and not so much used as experimented with, and anyway edited. The disputed object shrinks while the surrounding human labor expands to fill the frame. Marvel, defending the AI-generated title sequence of Secret Invasion, emphasized that no artists were replaced and that AI was one tool inside a human process; Coca-Cola, after its AI holiday advertisements were called hollow, foregrounded the human storytellers who had guided the machine. A lexicon assembles itself: generated becomes assisted; used becomes experimented with; made becomes guided. None of these descriptions is provably false. That is what makes scale itself argumentative: the grammar does the advocacy that the facts decline to do.

The maneuver has a somber twin. In February 2023, Vanderbilt's Peabody College sent students a condolence email about the Michigan State shooting — urging community, empathy, mutual care — with a small note at the bottom disclosing that the message had been written using ChatGPT. The apology that followed performed the customary contraction: the values remained ours; the failure was a discrete lapse of technological judgment. But the sting of the incident was never that a machine arranged the sentences — institutional prose was already a collective technology of templates and committee edits. The sting was that a condolence is a ritual of presence: the words matter because someone is supposed to have borne the difficulty of choosing them. The office had automated the evidence of having paused. And the machine completed the form so easily because the form was already empty — community, dialogue, ongoing commitment — warm words requiring no local referent. The model did not invent the hollow ceremony. It passed the ceremony's exam.

Primary record
  • Entertainment Weekly
  • Vanderbilt Hustler
Exhibit 7Exhibit G — Only Three Images
RETURN

The Unknown Returns

Back, finally, to the warehouse — because the Glasgow event left one loose end, and it is the whole case.

The Unknown: a villain no one will admit to inventing. The script produced the character (the organizer says he wrote the script; the actor says the machine did). The organizer supplied a warehouse. A teenage performer supplied a body and a silver mask. A mother supplied a video. The internet supplied an audience, meme accounts supplied repetition, and a production company proposed a horror film — a full afterlife for an entity whose parentage remains formally unclaimed. The name is almost too convenient: responsibility left the building disguised as an evil chocolate-maker called The Unknown.

So: who made The Unknown?

The one forbidden answer is "everyone," because everyone is where judgment goes to dissolve. The tribunal in Exhibit B already showed the better method, and it scales. Ask what relation each party controlled. The organizer controlled the promises, the venue, the ticket price, and the decision to hand performers whatever those fifteen pages were. The advertising system controlled the ease of conjuring sugared avenues no warehouse would honor. The actor controlled a performance under duress and, later, testimony. The parents controlled the filming; the platforms, the amplification; the audience, the circulation it condemned while circulating. These obligations differ. None of them cancels another. Responsibility is not a pie that thins as hands reach the table. Responsibility is the duty to answer for the relation one controlled — and adding actors to the scene does not dilute it; it multiplies the points where the sequence could have been stopped.

That is the standard every exhibit converges on. The airline answers for the agent it deployed; the lawyer answers for the citation he filed; the prankster answers for the caption he wrote, whatever the experiment revealed; the company that manufactures a grandmother answers for the stereotype it monetizes, however deserving the scammers; the newspaper answers for the masthead, whoever licensed the insert. Trace any of these backward far enough — prompt, model, dataset, market, history, hunger — and the word cause is eventually asked to hold a civilization, and splits at the spine. Let it split. Explanation has a moral limit. Past it, complexity stops clarifying and begins laundering. We do not need a theory large enough to make everyone innocent. We need a language precise enough to make each participant answerable for the power that passed through their hands.

The mystery, in the end, was never that the machine speaks. The mystery is how quickly we recognize ourselves in the speech — how eagerly we claim its grace, how violently we disown its appetite, how reliably we rename the whole transaction once we learn whether we admire, fear, owe, or regret what came out.

We call the voice inhuman at the exact moment it begins to pronounce our alibi correctly.

Primary record
  • WIRED
Exhibit 8The Unknown Returns

Image credits and treatment.

The opening case now carries a seven-image documentary dossier: two original AI promotional images, three views of the physical venue, a performer at the improvised laboratory table, and the viral appearance of the Unknown. All major figures use documentary photographs, published screenshots, editorial source imagery, or official study figures. Images are embedded directly for reliable offline reading and are individually credited for critical, scholarly discussion.

The Pronoun Alibi · Watson Hartsoe · July 2026Single-file · source-image edition · mobile-first · print-ready
